title: Building a rabbit nest box
subtitle: A simple plywood nest box for a kindling doe and her litter.
excerpt: A doe near her due date will pull fur from her belly and start carrying hay; she needs a nest box in her hutch by day twenty-five of pregnancy. This walks through a basic plywood box, the right dimensions, the bedding sequence, and when to put it in.
sourceNotes: Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, rabbit chapter. Cornell Small Farms meat-rabbit notes. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management, Project Gutenberg edition, rabbit-keeping chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: The rabbit term for giving birth. A kindling doe needs a quiet, deeply bedded nest box and the bucks separated; she nurses the litter once or twice a day rather than continuously.
A female rabbit. A doe in milk feeds her litter from about ten days before kindling onwards; a doe kept for breeding needs nest-box access from day twenty-five of her pregnancy.

content.text: A nest box is the rabbit equivalent of the farrowing pen: a quiet enclosed corner where the
doe

can pull her fur, settle her litter, and warm them through the first ten days of life. Without one, kits born onto the open hutch floor get cold and die overnight. The box needs to be in the hutch by day twenty-five of pregnancy at the latest; rabbits gestate for about thirty-one days, and a doe with no nest available kindles where she stands.
Dimensions

Aim for inside dimensions of thirty by twenty by twenty centimetres for a medium meat breed. The front opening is fifteen by fifteen centimetres. Raise the bottom edge of that opening eight centimetres off the floor. The doe steps over the lip; the young kits cannot tumble out. A larger breed gets a slightly larger box. A smaller breed gets a smaller one.

The box is open-topped for ventilation and easy access. A close-fitting lid is optional and useful for the first few days when the doe wants the litter dark; a piece of board the doe can push aside is enough.
Materials and cutting

Twelve-millimetre exterior plywood is the right choice. Cut one base (thirty by twenty), two sides (thirty by twenty), and two ends (twenty by twenty). One end gets the entrance opening cut out with a jigsaw; sand the cut edges smooth so the doe does not catch her ears. A few small drainage holes drilled in the base prevent any urine from puddling under the bedding.
Assembly

Glue and screw the box together using exterior PVA and twenty-five-millimetre wood screws. Pilot the holes to avoid splitting the ply. Counter-sink the screw heads slightly so they sit below the surface; a screw head proud of the wood is a hazard to a kit's eyes. Sand all internal surfaces smooth. Finish the outside with raw linseed oil or leave it bare; never paint or varnish the inside where the doe and kits will be in contact.
Bedding sequence

Add two layers in a particular order. The base layer is a generous handful of dry shavings, levelled across the base. The top layer is loose meadow hay packed into the corners and a deeper bed of soft hay in the middle. The doe rearranges the hay to suit her instinct, pulls fur from her belly to add to it, and bedded the centre with the result. Resist the urge to interfere with her arrangement once she has started; she knows what she wants.
When to put the box in
Day twenty-five to twenty-eight of pregnancy is the sweet spot. Earlier than that and the doe uses the box as a toilet, fouling the bedding before
kindling

. Later and she may have started without the box. Mark the date she was put to the buck so you know where she is in gestation; rabbits show no obvious physical signs of pregnancy until the last week, when the abdomen drops slightly.
After kindling

A doe kindles overnight or in the early morning, usually with no warning, and the litter is born into the bedded centre of the nest box. Look in once a day to check the kits are alive, warm, and have full bellies; lift the doe out briefly if she is reluctant to leave the box, otherwise let her be. Do not touch a kit with bare hands beyond the briefest check; the doe may reject a kit that smells of human hand. A clean glove dusted with shavings from the bedding masks the scent and is fine to use when you need a closer look.

Remove any dead kit from the box at the morning check; a doe will sometimes lose one or two from a large litter and leave them in the bedding. Replace any soiled bedding once the kits are ten days old and beginning to move around. The box stays in the hutch until the kits are four weeks old, when they spend most of their time outside it. Take the box out at five weeks.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Nest box problems
attrs.intro: Most first-litter problems are nest-box problems.

items.symptom: Doe is using the box as a toilet and not bedding it.
items.cause: Box put in too early, or the doe is not pregnant.
items.fix: Clean the box out and refresh the bedding. Check the breeding records; if she is past day twenty-eight without bedding behaviour, she may not have taken. Re-introduce to the buck after another week.

items.symptom: Kits scattered around the hutch floor at the morning check.
items.cause: Doe kindled outside the box, or she has been disturbed and moved them.
items.fix: Gather the live kits gently with a gloved hand and place them in the bedded box. Bed the box with extra fur if any pulled fur is on the hutch floor. Most does accept the rearrangement and nurse normally that evening.

items.symptom: Whole litter cold and stiff in the morning.
items.cause: Doe failed to pull fur, or kindled at a bad time and the kits chilled overnight.
items.fix: First-time does sometimes lose a litter; it does not mean she is a poor breeder. Note the loss in the breeding records, give her a month off, and try again. If a second litter goes the same way, retire her from breeding.

items.symptom: Doe biting and pulling at her kits.
items.cause: Stress, often from disturbance or noise near the hutch.
items.fix: Move the hutch to a quieter spot. Reduce visits to the once-a-day morning check. If she continues, remove the kits to a fostering doe if available; a doe rejecting her own litter is unusual but does happen.

content.text: Two or three nest boxes through the colony cover the breeding programme. Build them in a quiet afternoon, label one box per doe so the bedding scent is hers next time, and the same boxes serve for many years of litters.
